translation: After the travail of his soul, he will see [the result] and be satisfied; through his knowledge, the righteous servant will justify many, and their iniquities he will bear.

alternate_translations.translation: From the labor of his soul he will see his reward and be content; by his understanding, my righteous servant will make many righteous, and he will carry their iniquities.
alternate_translations.notes: “יִרְאֶה” can be rendered as “see” (i.e., observe/recognize the outcome), sometimes expanded as “see his reward.” “יַצְדִּיק” literally means “will justify,” but can also be expressed in English as “make … righteous,” which conveys the legal/forensic sense of declaring someone righteous.

alternate_translations.translation: He will behold the fruit of the travail of his soul and be satisfied. By what he knows, my righteous servant will bring many to righteousness, and he will bear their wrongdoings.
alternate_translations.notes: “עֲוֹנֹתָם” is “their iniquities/wrongdoings.” “בְּדַעְתּוֹ” (“in/through his knowledge”) is sometimes paraphrased as “by what he knows,” while keeping the instrumental sense.

notes: This is a prophetic/biblical Hebrew passage describing the “righteous servant” (עַבְדִּי) and the effect of his suffering (“travail of his soul”). The phrase “יַצְדִּיק צַדִּיק” emphasizes both the servant’s righteousness and his role in “justifying” (i.e., making/declaring others righteous in a legal-spiritual sense). “He will bear their iniquities” uses “bear” language that is often read as substitutionary or at least representative suffering.
